EVENT_INDOOR_DAYTIME.rationale: Conference rooms and daytime event spaces have decent ambient light. f/4 provides quick depth-of-field flexibility.
EVENT_INDOOR_DAYTIME.proTip: Arrive early to test the lighting in all key event areas to avoid surprises during the event.
EVENT_INDOOR_DAYTIME.commonMistake: Not scoping the venue in advance—discovering the lighting is terrible only when the event starts.

EVENT_INDOOR_LOW_LIGHT.rationale: Dim event venues need elevated ISO. Manual prevents hunting between bright and dark areas in the room.
EVENT_INDOOR_LOW_LIGHT.proTip: Pre-select your ISO before entering the venue and re-evaluate using the histogram when you arrive.
EVENT_INDOOR_LOW_LIGHT.commonMistake: Auto ISO hunts between the bright stage and dark audience, causing inconsistent exposure throughout the event.

EVENT_CONCERT_STAGE.rationale: Stage lighting changes rapidly and dramatically. Manual mode is essential. 1/250 freezes performer movement.
EVENT_CONCERT_STAGE.proTip: Most concerts allow photography of the first 3 songs only. Position yourself for the best angle before the show starts.
EVENT_CONCERT_STAGE.commonMistake: Not knowing the 'first 3 songs' rule and getting escorted out—know venue policies in advance.

EVENT_WEDDING_CEREMONY.rationale: Ceremonies vary from bright outdoor to dim chapel. Aperture priority with Auto ISO adapts automatically.
EVENT_WEDDING_CEREMONY.proTip: Communicate with the officiant before the ceremony—some prohibit movement during key moments like the vow exchange.
EVENT_WEDDING_CEREMONY.commonMistake: Moving and drawing attention during the ring exchange or kiss moments—be positioned early and stay still.

EVENT_CANDID_SHOTS.rationale: Candid moments require discretion. Fast lens at f/2 captures authentic expressions in any ambient lighting.
EVENT_CANDID_SHOTS.proTip: Use a 35mm or 50mm lens—longer telephotos draw attention and people stiffen up.
EVENT_CANDID_SHOTS.commonMistake: Using a big white telephoto lens at social events telegraphs your presence and kills candid moments.

EVENT_FLASH_PORTRAITS.rationale: Flash sync speed and f/8 creates consistently sharp, evenly lit portraits across a variety of ambient conditions.
EVENT_FLASH_PORTRAITS.proTip: Use a Rogue FlashBender or bounce card on your flash to soften and direct light more naturally.
EVENT_FLASH_PORTRAITS.commonMistake: Pointing the flash directly at faces—bounce flash off the ceiling or use a diffuser for natural-looking results.

EVENT_RECEPTION_LOW_LIGHT.rationale: Wedding receptions are notoriously dark. ISO 6400 is often the minimum needed for acceptable shutter speeds.
EVENT_RECEPTION_LOW_LIGHT.proTip: A prime lens (35mm or 50mm f/1.4) gives you 1.5–2 stops advantage over a zoom in dark receptions.
EVENT_RECEPTION_LOW_LIGHT.commonMistake: Depending entirely on flash for reception shots—ambient light shots tell a more authentic story.

EVENT_OUTDOOR_NIGHT.rationale: Outdoor night events use string lights, uplighting, and torches. Find the best-lit spots and work them.
EVENT_OUTDOOR_NIGHT.proTip: Festival string lights and lanterns create beautiful bokeh in the background—position subjects near light strings.
EVENT_OUTDOOR_NIGHT.commonMistake: Using flash outdoors at night erases all the beautiful ambient lighting that makes outdoor night events magical.

EVENT_SPEECH_STAGE.rationale: Stage podium lighting is controlled and consistent. Manual maintains exposure through the speech.
EVENT_SPEECH_STAGE.proTip: Capture the speaker at decisive moments—hand gesture peaks, strong facial expressions, and audience reactions.
EVENT_SPEECH_STAGE.commonMistake: Taking frames at random instead of waiting for and anticipating the peak expressive moments.

EVENT_PARTY_FAST_MOVEMENT.rationale: Energetic party scenes with dancing require fast shutters at high ISO. Continuous tracking maintains focus.
EVENT_PARTY_FAST_MOVEMENT.proTip: Use off-camera flash remotely triggered at low power for a fill-light that adds depth without killing the mood.
EVENT_PARTY_FAST_MOVEMENT.commonMistake: Full-power on-camera flash freezes the room and removes all energy from party photos.

EVENT_BLACK_WHITE.rationale: B&W event photography is timeless and hides noise from high ISO. Emotion and story over technical perfection.
EVENT_BLACK_WHITE.proTip: High ISO noise actually looks like film grain in B&W—embrace it for a photojournalistic quality.
EVENT_BLACK_WHITE.commonMistake: Obsessing over noise at high ISO instead of getting the shot—noisy B&W is better than missed color shot.

EVENT_REFLECTIONS.rationale: Mirrored surfaces in event spaces create dramatic double-image effects that tell richer stories.
EVENT_REFLECTIONS.proTip: Event venues often have polished floors, glass walls, and mirrors—use them for creative angles.
EVENT_REFLECTIONS.commonMistake: Missing the reflective opportunities that high-end venues offer—look down as often as looking forward.

EVENT_GROUP_SHOTS.rationale: f/5.6 ensures the entire group is sharp. Burst mode captures at least one frame with all eyes open.
EVENT_GROUP_SHOTS.proTip: Count to 3 loudly before shooting—this focuses people's attention and prevents blinks at the critical moment.
EVENT_GROUP_SHOTS.commonMistake: Taking just one shot of a group—it's virtually guaranteed someone will blink or look away.

EVENT_BACKSTAGE.rationale: Backstage areas are typically the darkest spaces—maximum aperture and IBIS critical for handheld authenticity.
EVENT_BACKSTAGE.proTip: Backstage is where the authentic, unguarded moments happen—document preparations, candid interactions, and genuine emotion.
EVENT_BACKSTAGE.commonMistake: Using flash backstage broadcasts your presence—IBIS and high ISO maintain the authentic atmosphere.

EVENT_RED_CARPET.rationale: Red carpet backdrops are consistently lit. Manual mode gives consistent exposure across every subject.
EVENT_RED_CARPET.proTip: Pre-focus on the backdrop at the correct distance and lock it—all subjects stand in the same spot.
EVENT_RED_CARPET.commonMistake: Letting subjects move off the mark—politely position them on the marked spot for consistent results.
